nativeRange.habitat: The Milk Spotted Puffer inhabits coastal estuaries, river mouths, mangrove swamps, lagoons, and shallow reef-associated areas across its range. It is typically encountered at depths of about 4 to 60 metres, generally staying within a few kilometres of the sea.

diet.notes: Generally considered a meaty-food carnivore. Has been documented feeding on crustaceans and molluscs in the wild. In captivity, has been known to accept frozen shrimp, krill, clams, mussels, squid, and the occasional live feeder shrimp. Hard-shelled prey such as snails and shell-on shrimp are commonly advised to help wear down the species' continuously growing beak-like teeth.

summary: The Milk Spotted Puffer (Chelonodontops patoca) is a large Indo-Pacific puffer from coastal estuaries, recognizable by its grey body mottled with dark markings and milky-white spots. It is commonly considered semi-aggressive and is best kept singly in a fish-only system.
careNotes:
Chelonodontops patoca — often still listed under its older synonym Chelonodon patoca in the aquarium trade — is a euryhaline puffer that occurs across the Indo-West Pacific, from East Africa east to the Admiralty and Trobriand Islands, north to China and south to northern Australia. Euryhaline means the species can tolerate a wide range of salinities. It tends to inhabit coastal estuaries, mangroves, lagoons, and river mouths; juveniles are commonly encountered in brackish water and occasionally swim some distance up freshwater streams, while adults are typically found in higher-salinity coastal habitats. Although a salinity range of roughly SG 1.005–1.025 has been reported for the species in the wild, most care guides advise keeping it in full marine conditions (around SG 1.020–1.025).

Minimum tank size recommendations vary across sources, ranging from about 380 L to 473 L (roughly 100–125 US gallons); 400 L is a reasonable working figure that accommodates an active swimmer reaching up to 38 cm.

Like all members of family Tetraodontidae, the Milk Spotted Puffer can inflate its body by ingesting water when threatened and should never be allowed to inhale air during handling. A container rather than a net is generally advised for transfers. The flesh and organs contain tetrodotoxin and are highly toxic to eat, though the species is not generally documented to release skin toxins lethal to tankmates in the way boxfishes or soapfishes can. The Milk Spotted Puffer is commonly considered semi-aggressive, and most care guidance advises keeping it singly in a fish-only system with robust tankmates, avoiding crustaceans, molluscs, corals, and small or slow-moving fish. Captive breeding has not been reported.
breedingNotes: No captive-breeding protocols for the Milk Spotted Puffer have been documented. In the wild the species reproduces by laying eggs, but nothing is known about the specifics of spawning or larval care in an aquarium setting.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Care sheet: ~13 in adult size, 100 gal minimum, 72–80°F, pH 7.8–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, semi-aggressive, not reef safe, carnivore.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Tetraodontidae/Tetraodontiformes), maximum size (38 cm SL), depth range (4–60 m), Indo-Pacific distribution, brackish/marine/freshwater tolerance, temperature 23–28°C, poisonous-to-eat status, oviparous.

additional.name: Aqua Imports
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (125 gal), adult size (10 in), 75–80°F, pH 7.5–8.5, brackish-to-marine, calm-to-semi-aggressive, carnivore with shell-on prey. Tank size averaged with LiveAquaria's 100 gal and rounded up to 400 L.

additional.name: Wikipedia / hobby summary (Chelonodontops patoca)
additional.notes: Taxonomy fallback: confirmed common-name synonyms (milkspotted toadfish, marbled toad, Gangetic pufferfish/blowfish) and Indo-Pacific range; brackish tolerance ~SG 1.005–1.025 noted.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented. The species lays eggs in the wild.
